Completed the Şehzade Mosque in Istanbul, which he considered his first major work.
Completed the Süleymaniye Mosque in Istanbul, a symbol of the Ottoman golden age.
Completed the Selimiye Mosque in Edirne, which he regarded as his masterpiece.
Died in Istanbul at an advanced age and was buried in a tomb of his own design near the Süleymaniye Mosque.
Süleymaniye Mosque (Istanbul)
A vast complex and architectural marvel that became a symbol of the power and grandeur of Sultan Suleiman's reign.
Selimiye Mosque (Edirne)
Considered by Sinan himself to be his masterpiece, perfecting the centralized dome structure and creating a vast, unified interior space.
Mehmed Paša Sokolović Bridge (Višegrad)
A UNESCO World Heritage site, this elegant bridge showcases his genius in civil engineering and harmonious integration with the landscape.
Prolific Builder
Credited with the design and construction of over 300 major structures, including 92 mosques, 52 chapels, 55 schools, 20 caravanserais, and 35 palaces.
How many buildings did Mimar Sinan design?
He is credited with being responsible for over 300 structures, including mosques, palaces, bridges, aqueducts, and schools, though he directly designed and supervised a smaller number of these.
What was Mimar Sinan's background?

He was born a Christian (likely Cappadocian Greek or Armenian) and was recruited into the Ottoman state through the Devşirme system, where he converted to Islam and trained as a military engineer before becoming an architect.
What is considered Mimar Sinan's masterpiece?
Sinan himself considered the Selimiye Mosque in Edirne to be his masterpiece, representing the pinnacle of his architectural achievements and the culmination of his life's work.
How did Sinan's military background influence his work?

His experience as a military engineer gave him profound practical knowledge of logistics, materials, and structural stability. This foundation in engineering allowed him to push the boundaries of architectural design, creating massive domes and stable structures on a grand scale.
Born in Hamadan, Persia (modern-day Iran).
Leaves Hamadan and travels through the Buyid empire, seeking knowledge and patronage.
Arrives in Nishapur and famously debates the scholar Abu Bakr al-Khwarizmi, earning his title 'Badi' al-Zaman'.
Composes his most famous work, the Maqamat, inventing a new literary genre.
Dies in Herat (in modern-day Afghanistan) at the young age of 38.
Invention of the Maqama
He created the literary genre of the Maqama, episodic stories in rhyming prose (saj') featuring a witty rogue and a narrator.
Mastery of Arabic Prose
His works are considered pinnacles of Arabic literary style, demonstrating unparalleled command of vocabulary, rhetoric, and rhyming prose.
Influence on Arabic Literature
His Maqamat became a model for subsequent writers for centuries, most notably al-Hariri of Basra.
What does 'Badi' al-Zaman' mean?
It is an honorific title in Arabic meaning 'The Wonder of the Age,' given to him after he won a famous literary debate.
Was al-Hamadani Arab or Persian?
He was of Persian origin, born in Hamadan, but he was educated in and became a master of the Arabic language, which was the lingua franca of scholarship and literature at the time.
What is the main theme of his Maqamat?
The Maqamat uses the adventures of a clever vagabond to satirize society, display linguistic virtuosity, and explore themes of deception, survival, and the power of words.
Born in Nurs, Bitlis Vilayet, Ottoman Empire.
Travels to Istanbul, proposes educational reforms to combine religious and modern sciences.
Exiled to Burdur, then the remote village of Barla, beginning a 25-year period of exile and imprisonment.
Writes the majority of the Risale-i Nur collection while in exile in Barla.
Faces his first major trial and imprisonment in Eskişehir, along with many of his students.
Dies in Urfa, Turkey. His grave was later moved to an unknown location by the military government.
The Risale-i Nur Collection
Authored a 6,000-page Qur'anic commentary that addresses modern questions of faith, science, and philosophy.
The Nur Movement
Inspired a major non-political Islamic faith movement in Turkey and worldwide, focused on education and spiritual renewal.
Reconciliation of Faith and Reason
Developed a methodology to defend religious truths using logical proofs and arguments compatible with modern science.
What does 'Bediüzzaman' mean?
It means 'The Wonder of the Age,' a title given to him in his youth for his exceptional intelligence, photographic memory, and debating skills.
Was Said Nursi a political figure?
He was involved in public life in his youth but later adopted a stance of being 'averse to politics,' focusing entirely on spiritual and faith-based service through his writings.
What is the main message of the Risale-i Nur?
Its central purpose is to prove the truths of the Qur'an and Islamic faith using rational arguments and observations from the natural world, countering the materialism and atheism of the modern era.
Why was he persecuted?

The staunchly secularist Turkish Republic viewed his influential religious teachings and the growing community around him as a potential threat to its secular ideology, leading to his constant exile, surveillance, and imprisonment.
Born a Kipchak Turk on the Pontic-Caspian Steppe.
Sold as a slave in Syria, eventually purchased by the Ayyubid sultan of Egypt.
Plays a key role in defeating the Seventh Crusade at the Battle of Al Mansurah.
Leads the vanguard of the Mamluk army to a decisive victory over the Mongols at the Battle of Ain Jalut.
Assassinates Sultan Qutuz and ascends to the throne as the Mamluk Sultan.
Captures the Crusader city of Antioch, a major blow to the Crusader states.
Captures the formidable Crusader castles of Krak des Chevaliers and Montfort.
Dies in Damascus, possibly from poison.
Victory at Ain Jalut
Halted the seemingly unstoppable Mongol advance into the Middle East, a pivotal moment in world history.
Dismantling Crusader States
Systematically conquered key Crusader strongholds like Antioch and Krak des Chevaliers, effectively ending their power in the Levant.
Consolidation of the Mamluk Empire
Reformed the army, built infrastructure like canals and bridges, and established a postal network, creating a stable and powerful state.
Why is Baybars called the 'true founder' of the Mamluk Sultanate?

While he was the fourth sultan, his military victories, administrative reforms, and consolidation of power transformed the Mamluk realm into a stable and dominant empire, laying the foundation for its 250-year rule.
What does his name, al-Bunduqdari, mean?
It means 'the crossbowman,' referring to the master who first purchased him as a slave, the amir Aydakin al-Bunduqdar.
Was Baybars a cruel ruler?

He was known for his ruthlessness against enemies and political rivals, including assassinating his predecessor. However, as a ruler, he was also known for his justice, piety, and extensive public works that benefited his people.
Born in Shiraz, Persia.
Leaves Shiraz to study in Baghdad at the Nizamiyya University following the Mongol invasions.
Embarks on a thirty-year journey across the Middle East, North Africa, and Anatolia.
Completes his epic poem 'Bustan' (The Orchard).
Completes his most famous work, 'Gulistan' (The Rose Garden).
Dies in his hometown of Shiraz.
Gulistan (The Rose Garden)
Authored this masterpiece of prose and poetry, which offers moral and social guidance through anecdotes and is one of the most influential works in Persian literature.
Bustan (The Orchard)
Composed this long epic poem entirely in verse, focusing on virtues like justice, humility, and contentment, solidifying his status as a master moralist.
Influence on Global Literature
His works were among the first pieces of Persian literature to be translated into Western languages, influencing figures like Voltaire, Goethe, and Ralph Waldo Emerson.
Why is Saadi so famous?

Saadi is renowned for his two major works, 'Gulistan' and 'Bustan', which masterfully blend entertaining anecdotes, practical advice, and profound moral philosophy in an accessible and elegant style that has resonated with readers for centuries.
What does the name 'Saadi' mean?
He adopted the pen name 'Saadi' in honor of the local Atabeg (ruler) of Fars, Sa'd ibn Zangi, who was a patron of his.
What is his most famous quote?
His most famous verse, part of his 'Gulistan', is 'The children of Adam are limbs of one body...'. A version of this poem is inscribed at the entrance of the United Nations building in New York.
c. 2334 BCE
Rises to power, overthrows Ur-Zababa of Kish, and founds the city of Akkad.
Conducts a series of military campaigns, conquering Sumerian city-states like Uruk, Ur, and Lagash.
c. 2300 BCE
Establishes the Akkadian Empire, uniting Mesopotamia and creating the world's first known empire.
c. 2279 BCE
Dies after a 56-year reign, passing the empire to his son, Rimush.
Creation of the First Empire
He conquered and united the disparate city-states of Mesopotamia, forming the world's first known multinational empire.
Military Innovation
He established a standing army of 5,400 soldiers, a novel concept that allowed for sustained military campaigns.
He standardized weights, measures, and the calendar, and made the Akkadian language the official tongue of administration, facilitating trade and governance.
Was Sargon's birth story true?
It is considered a legend, likely created to legitimize his rule by suggesting divine favor and obscuring his non-royal origins. The story is very similar to the later biblical story of Moses.
What does the name 'Sargon' mean?
The name 'Sargon' (Šarru-kīn in Akkadian) means 'the legitimate king' or 'the true king'. He likely adopted this name upon taking the throne to assert his right to rule.
How did the Akkadian Empire fall?

After Sargon's death, the empire faced constant rebellions. While his successors held it together for a time, a combination of internal strife, external attacks (notably by the Gutians), and possibly climate-induced famine led to its collapse around 2154 BCE.
Born to Ardashir I, the founder of the Sasanian Empire.
Ascends to the throne as the second Sasanian King of Kings.
Defeats the Roman army under Emperor Gordian III at the Battle of Misiche.
Conducts his second campaign against Rome, sacking the major city of Antioch.
Achieves his most famous victory at the Battle of Edessa, defeating and capturing the Roman Emperor Valerian.
Founds the city of Bishapur, using captured Roman soldiers and engineers for its construction.
Dies, leaving behind a greatly expanded and strengthened empire.
Capture of Emperor Valerian
He achieved the unprecedented feat of defeating and capturing a Roman emperor, Valerian, a humiliation for Rome immortalized in Sasanian rock reliefs.
Imperial Expansion and Consolidation
He expanded the Sasanian Empire's territory into Roman Syria and Armenia and consolidated his father's conquests.
Founder of Cities
He founded several major cities, including Bishapur and Gundeshapur, which became important cultural, administrative, and intellectual centers.
What happened to the captured Emperor Valerian?

Historical accounts vary. Sasanian sources claim he was treated with respect, while Roman sources suggest he was humiliated, possibly used as a human footstool by Shapur. He died in captivity, and his skills, along with those of his soldiers, were used for construction projects in Persia.
What was Shapur I's religious policy?

Shapur I was a Zoroastrian but practiced a policy of religious tolerance. He notably protected the prophet Mani, founder of Manichaeism, and allowed Christian and Jewish communities to thrive within his empire.
What are the Naqsh-e Rostam reliefs?

They are a series of large rock reliefs carved into a cliffside in Iran. Shapur I commissioned a famous relief depicting his triumph over three Roman emperors: Gordian III, Philip the Arab, and the captured Valerian.
Born in Constantinople, son of Sultan Mustafa III.
Ascends to the Ottoman throne during the Austro-Turkish and Russo-Turkish wars.
Signs the Treaty of Jassy, ending the war with Russia and allowing him to focus on domestic reforms.
Formally establishes the 'Nizam-ı Cedid' (New Order) to modernize the state, starting with a new army.
Faces the French invasion of Ottoman Egypt and Syria, led by Napoleon Bonaparte.
Deposed by a Janissary revolt led by Kabakçı Mustafa, and his reforms are abolished.
Assassinated in the Topkapı Palace on the orders of his successor, Mustafa IV, during an attempt to restore him to the throne.
Nizam-ı Cedid (New Order) Army
Established a modern, European-trained infantry corps to challenge the power of the traditional Janissaries.
Diplomatic Modernization
Opened the first permanent Ottoman embassies in major European capitals like London, Paris, and Vienna.
Patronage of Arts and Music
Was an accomplished poet, calligrapher, and musician who composed many works, fostering a cultural flourishing during his reign.
What was the Nizam-ı Cedid?

It was a series of reforms in the late 18th century aimed at modernizing the Ottoman Empire. Its centerpiece was the creation of a new Western-style army, but it also included fiscal, administrative, and diplomatic changes.
Why did the Janissaries oppose Selim III?
They saw his new army, the Nizam-ı Cedid, as a direct threat to their centuries-old military dominance, political influence, and privileged status within the empire.
Was Selim III's reign a complete failure?

While his reforms were overturned and he was killed, his efforts were the first major attempt at Western-style modernization. His reign is seen as a pivotal, though tragic, turning point that laid the groundwork for the subsequent Tanzimat reforms.
Born in Herat, modern-day Afghanistan.
Ascends the Safavid throne at the age of 16 amidst a military coup against his father.
Signs a harsh peace treaty with the Ottoman Empire, ceding large territories to buy time for internal reforms.
Moves the capital from Qazvin to Isfahan and defeats the Uzbeks near Herat, securing the eastern frontier.
Wages a successful war against the Ottoman Empire, reclaiming territories including Baghdad, Azerbaijan, and Georgia.
With the help of the English navy, captures the strategic island of Hormuz from the Portuguese.
Recaptures Baghdad from the Ottomans.
Dies in Mazandaran, leaving a powerful but unstable legacy due to the lack of a capable heir.
Military Reform
He created the 'ghulam' corps, a standing army of elite slave soldiers loyal only to the Shah, breaking the power of the tribal Qizilbash.
Reconquest of Territories
He successfully waged wars against the Ottoman Empire and the Uzbeks, restoring Persia's traditional borders.
Isfahan's Golden Age
He transformed Isfahan into a magnificent capital, a global center of art, architecture, and commerce, epitomized by Naqsh-e Jahan Square.
Economic Prosperity
He revitalized the Persian economy by monopolizing and promoting the silk trade, building infrastructure, and ensuring public safety on trade routes.
Why is Abbas I known as 'the Great'?
He is called 'the Great' for restoring Persia's military and political strength, expanding its borders, and presiding over a golden age of culture and economy.
What was his relationship with his sons?
Tragically, he grew paranoid of conspiracies. He had his eldest son and heir, Mohammad Baqer Mirza, executed and had two other sons blinded, leaving no capable successor to the throne.
Who were the 'ghulams'?
The ghulams were an elite military corps composed of converted and trained Circassians, Georgians, and Armenians. They were fiercely loyal to the Shah personally, not to tribal or ethnic factions.
How did he interact with Europe?

He actively sought alliances with European powers, such as Spain and England, to create a second front against their common enemy, the Ottoman Empire. This led to increased diplomatic and commercial contact between Persia and Europe.
c. 685 BCE
Born in Nineveh, a younger son of King Esarhaddon.
c. 669 BCE
Ascends to the throne of Assyria after the death of his father.
Conducts major campaigns in Egypt, culminating in the sack of Thebes.
c. 652 BCE
A devastating civil war begins with his older brother, Shamash-shum-ukin, ruler of Babylon.
c. 648 BCE
Defeats his brother and sacks the city of Babylon, ending the civil war.
c. 646 BCE
Sacks the Elamite capital of Susa, effectively destroying the rival kingdom of Elam.
c. 631 BCE
Dies, leaving the Assyrian Empire at its peak but severely overstretched and internally weakened.
The Library of Ashurbanipal
Assembled the first systematically collected and cataloged library in the ancient world, preserving over 30,000 cuneiform tablets.
Consolidation of the Empire
Maintained the Neo-Assyrian Empire at its peak territorial extent, stretching from the Caucasus Mountains to Egypt and from Cyprus to western Iran.
Conquest of Elam
Decisively defeated the long-time rival kingdom of Elam, sacking its capital Susa and ending its threat to Mesopotamia.
Was Ashurbanipal really literate?
Yes. Uniquely for an Assyrian king of his time, he boasted in his inscriptions of his ability to read and write complex cuneiform scripts, including archaic Sumerian.
What happened to the Assyrian Empire after his death?
The empire, weakened by decades of civil war and constant campaigning, rapidly declined and collapsed. Within two decades of his death, Nineveh was sacked and the empire ceased to exist.
How was his library rediscovered?

The ruins of his library were discovered in the mid-19th century by British archaeologist Austen Henry Layard at the site of ancient Nineveh, in modern-day Iraq. The tablets found there are the foundation of modern Assyriology.
What is his most famous depiction in art?
He is famously depicted in palace reliefs in a scene known as the 'Garden Party', where he reclines on a couch while the severed head of the Elamite king Teumman hangs from a nearby tree.
Born in Cairo, Egypt, to a wealthy family of mixed Turkish, Arab, Circassian, Kurdish, and Greek roots.
Graduated from the School of Law and was sent by Khedive Tewfik to study in France for three years.
Returned to Egypt and was appointed to a prominent position in the court of Khedive Abbas II.
Forced into exile by the British authorities due to his nationalist poetry and support for Egyptian independence. He chose to live in Andalusia, Spain.
Returned to Egypt from exile and was hailed as a national hero.
Crowned 'Amir al-Shu'ara' (The Prince of Poets) by his peers in a grand ceremony, acknowledging his status as the foremost poet in the Arab world.
Died in Cairo at the age of 64.
Prince of Poets (Amir al-Shu'ara)
Officially crowned by his peers in 1927, recognizing him as the greatest contemporary Arabic-language poet.
Pioneer of Arabic Poetic Drama
He introduced and established the genre of poetic drama in modern Arabic literature with plays like 'The Death of Cleopatra' and 'Majnun Laila'.
Modernization of Arabic Poetry
He blended classical Arabic poetic forms with European influences, revitalizing the language and themes of Arabic poetry for the modern era.
National Voice of Egypt
His patriotic poems inspired the Egyptian nationalist movement and solidified his role as a cultural and political icon.
Why was he called the 'Prince of Poets'?

The title 'Amir al-Shu'ara' was bestowed upon him in 1927 by a committee of Arab poets and writers to honor his unparalleled contributions, mastery of the Arabic language, and his role in reviving and modernizing Arabic poetry.
What was the impact of his exile in Spain?

His exile in Andalusia (Spain) was a period of great creative output. It inspired some of his most powerful and nostalgic works, where he lamented his separation from Egypt and drew parallels with the lost glory of the Arab empire in Spain.
What are his most famous works?
His most famous works include his collection of poems 'Al-Shawqiyyat', and his pioneering poetic plays such as 'The Death of Cleopatra', 'Majnun Laila', and 'Cambyses'.
Is he still relevant today?

Yes, Shawqi is considered a foundational figure of modern Arabic literature. His poems are studied in schools across the Arab world, and his innovations in poetic drama and lyrical poetry continue to influence writers today.
Born in Lofça (now Lovech, Bulgaria).
Arrived in Constantinople to pursue higher education.
Appointed official historian of the Ottoman Empire.
Appointed head of the Mecelle Commission to codify Ottoman civil law.
Served his first of several terms as Minister of Justice.
Died in Constantinople.
The Mecelle (Ottoman Civil Code)
Led the commission that codified Hanafi law into the first systematic civil code of the Ottoman Empire, influencing law across the Muslim world.
Tarih-i Cevdet (History of Cevdet)
Authored a monumental 12-volume history of the Ottoman Empire from 1774 to 1826, a foundational text for modern Turkish historiography.
Tanzimat Reforms
Played a pivotal role as a statesman and intellectual in the 19th-century modernization reforms of the Ottoman state and society.
What is the Mecelle?

The Mecelle (Mecelle-i Ahkâm-ı Adliyye) was the civil code of the Ottoman Empire in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. It was the first attempt to codify a part of the Sharia-based law for a modern state.
Was Ahmed Cevdet Pasha a conservative or a reformer?

He was a unique figure who sought a synthesis. He believed in adopting Western-style institutions but insisted they must be grounded in and compatible with Islamic tradition, making him a 'conservative reformer'.
What was his most important contribution besides the Mecelle?
His 12-volume historical work, 'Tarih-i Cevdet', is considered a masterpiece of Ottoman historiography and a crucial source for understanding the empire's transition into the modern era.
Born in Ray, Persia (modern-day Iran).
Appointed chief physician of the hospital in Ray.
Moved to Baghdad and was appointed director of the main hospital, which he located using an experiment involving meat decomposition.
Wrote 'A Treatise on Smallpox and Measles' (Kitab al-Judari wa al-Hasbah), the first text to clinically differentiate the two diseases.
Died in his hometown of Ray. His students later compiled his notes into the 'Kitab al-Hawi' (The Comprehensive Book).
Differentiating Smallpox and Measles
He was the first to provide a clear clinical description and distinction between the two infectious diseases.
Kitab al-Hawi (The Comprehensive Book)
Authored a monumental medical encyclopedia that synthesized Greek, Syrian, and early Arabic medical knowledge, influencing medicine for centuries.
Pioneer of Experimental Medicine
He used controlled experiments, such as hanging meat to determine the most sanitary location for a hospital in Baghdad.
Contributions to Chemistry/Alchemy
He systematically classified chemical substances, described numerous chemical processes, and designed and described over twenty laboratory instruments.
Was Rhazes only a physician?
No, he was a polymath who wrote over 200 works on a wide range of subjects, including philosophy, alchemy, music, ethics, and theology, in addition to his extensive medical writings.
How did he choose the location for the Baghdad hospital?

He famously hung pieces of fresh meat in various locations around the city and chose the spot where the meat decomposed the slowest, correctly deducing it was the most hygienic and least contaminated area.
What was his most important medical book?

While his treatise on smallpox and measles was groundbreaking, his most extensive and influential work is the 'Kitab al-Hawi' (The Comprehensive Book), a massive encyclopedia of medical knowledge compiled from his notes after his death.
Born in Baghdad, son of the fifth Abbasid Caliph, Harun al-Rashid.
